questions.prompt: Why do analysts prefer EV/EBITDA over P/E when comparing firms with very different capital structures?
choices: EBITDA is always larger than earnings, making the multiple smaller
EV/EBITDA is capital-structure-neutral; P/E is distorted by interest expense and the share of value owed to debt
questions.explanation: EV adds back debt and EBITDA strips out interest and taxes, so both leveraged and unleveraged firms become comparable. P/E mixes operating performance with financing choice.

questions.prompt: An early-stage biotech with negative earnings can still be valued via which multiple?
questions.explanation: EV/Sales is the multiple of last resort when earnings are negative or volatile. It doesn't reflect margin but it always exists.

questions.prompt: A target firm has $80M of forecast EBITDA and $200M of net debt. Peer median EV/EBITDA = 12×. With 50M shares outstanding, what is the implied share price?
Which of these are common ways multiples mislead?
choices: Cyclical firm at the peak of its cycle looks 'cheap' on P/E
Lumping fast-growers with slow-growers and comparing P/E without adjusting
Using transaction multiples (with control premium) to value a public-market stake
Reporting the median multiple instead of the mean
questions.explanation: Cyclical traps, growth differences, and confusing transaction with trading multiples are all classic errors. Using median (not mean) is good practice — it resists outliers.
